summary: Shaw proposes a detailed consumer privacy law that would require companies to disclose which employees can access user accounts, maintain visible access logs, and let users block specific employees from viewing their data. The proposal includes mandatory credential-access disclosures at login screens, monthly access activity notifications, and requirements that company agents identify themselves when interacting with users. Shaw acknowledges the proposal is technically feasible but politically impossible, as companies have no incentive to reveal their internal access practices. He concludes with cynical realism that even if consumers had this information, most wouldn't act on it.
key_points: Login screens should prominently display which companies' employees can view your credentials and account data, not buried in privacy policies
Companies should provide access logs showing the name, company, and reason for every employee who accessed a user's account
Account holders should be able to explicitly block named employees from accessing their accounts, with fines for violations
Company employees interacting with users must be identified as agents of the company to prevent covert manipulation
The proposal is technically trivial but politically impossible because companies profit from unrestricted internal access to user data
money_quotes: I remember one company I worked at had an employee who routinely scanned people's files looking for juicy bits of information he could view.
This means that you now know that an employee at Google has the ability to look up your Wifi password, drive a car near your house, and log into your network to packet sniff your data.
They always allow their employees unrestricted access until they get their ass sued off over it.
If privacy is important, then it stands to reason that knowing who is potentially violating it is an important part of managing your personal security.
There's nothing radical or onerous about what's proposed. It's actually just good security practice at any company to restrict access to accounts.
Privacy and security have been so fully eroded in internet culture that even when given this information consumers wouldn't care.
If a law like this were even proposed you would see a cash tsunami rain down on Washington DC like it was a Thai fishing village.
key_insight: The basic security practice of logging and disclosing who accesses user accounts is technically trivial but will never be implemented because companies profit from the opacity.
